title: Judge rules AI evidence 'bordered on contempt'
full_text:
A judge has warned that workers' use of artificial intelligence in dismissal claims is "perilously close" to contempt of court after one claimant's chatbot cited large swaths of evidence that did not exist, in a case showing the technology bedevilling the workplace umpire is now hitting the courts.
In "a cautionary tale about the dangers of befriending AI-powered chatbots who masquerade as legal advisers", the Federal Circuit Court said a delivery driver's reliance on generative AI ruined his dismissal case against auto parts store Sterling Parts and put him at risk of fines and imprisonment.
The judgment represents the strongest words yet by the federal courts on AI in dismissal cases, which the Fair Work Commission has complained is producing incoherent claims that suck up resources and delay cases.
In the case, worker Weiman Ba cited 38 pieces of evidence - including policies, cost records, agreements, screenshots and photographs - that were integral to his claim that he had been incorrectly classified as an independent contractor, not an employee.
But after three months, multiple hearings, defences and applications at "significant cost" to the two employer respondents - both represented by separate law firms - the court found AI had hallucinated the evidence.
"While neither respondent invited the court to view the conduct of Ba as involving a contempt of court, the case comes perilously close to engaging this jurisdiction and the penal sanctions which can potentially apply," Judge Catherine Symons said.
"The reputation and authority of the court as a trusted institution is undermined when false or misleading 'evidence' is sought to be introduced and relied on in proceedings before it."
Dismissal claims under general protections, which have broader grounds than unfair dismissal and uncapped compensation, are estimated to have increased by 50 per cent in 2025-26 to about 9300 claims, according to FWC data. That is 80 per cent above their five-year average.
But the claims are only mediated in the commission and many later proceed to the federal courts. Data from federal courts shows employment claims rose by 60 per cent between 2022-25 and self-represented litigants in such matters jumped 200 per cent in 2024-25 in the Federal Court.
In judge Symons' case, Sterling Parts had initially requested Ba provide the evidentiary documents as it could not find them and needed them to plead its defence. However, Ba refused.
In response to court orders to do so, Ba simply responded by attaching the evidence list, which the companies argued was a "flagrant breach".
At that point, a lawyer for the companies said he suspected the "industry expert" Ba claimed to be taking advice from was actually an AI chatbot that had produced the evidence citations.
When Sterling Parts succeeded in getting the court to issue Ba a notice to produce the evidentiary documents - after the court threatened to dismiss Ba's case if he did not comply - Ba responded that the orders should not have been made.
Judge Symons said Ba was "deliberately obtuse" and his response amounted to "the continuation of a pattern of obstruction and prevarication".
She inferred from the doubling down on his refusal that his evidence was generated by an AI tool and the documents did not exist.
The judge warned there were "significant difficulties when AI answers a job description for legal adviser" given AI was not a lawyer, did not hold a practising certificate and was not authorised to provide legal advice.
"It owes no duty to the court or the administration of justice and owes no duty to act in the best interests of Ba or to observe regulatory or ethical standards, including in its engagement with other practitioners and parties," she said. "In other words, any AI tool used by Ba did not care as to the truth or otherwise of any material that it might have generated.
"The sycophantic tendency of large language models to prioritise user approval over truth can make them especially dangerous and unpredictable unless careful oversight, calibration and verification is applied."
Dismissing his case, the judge said the consequence of Ba's "blind faith in his AI 'legal expert' meant he will not have the benefit of the court's adjudication of this question".
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: David Marin-Guzman | Workplace corr
heading: Data Science and AI
